MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS (TRANSACTION ID # 200025804) FILED BY DEFENDANT SINGH, GURJIT HEARING SET FOR MAR-22-2021 AT 09:30 AM IN DEPT 302

LAW AND MOTION, 302, DEFENDANT GURJIT SINGH'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS. MATTER CALLED, NO ARGUMENT IS HELD. THE COURT INFORMS MR. MAXWELL THAT HE DID NOT TIMELY OPPOSE THE TENTATIVE RULING AND THAT OPPOSING COUNSEL IS NOT PRESENT. THE COURT THEN ADOPTED THE TENTATIVE RULING AS FOLLOWS: SPECIALLY APPEARING DEFENDANT GURJIT SINGH'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS AND TO SET ASIDE THE DEFAULT ENTERED AGAINST HIM ON DECEMBER 16, 2020 IS DENIED. COUNSEL FOR THE PREVAILING PARTY IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH REPEATS VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE TENTATIVE RULING AND THAT IS IN COMPLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN; CLERK: JACQUELINE ALAMEDA; COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR NO. 8600, EMAIL: MARIA.TORREANO@GMAIL.COM. (302/EPS)

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Mar-22-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances (via ZOOM videoconference):
Atty. Nicholas Maxwell, Esq. of Bassi Edlin Huie & Blum, 415.403.4422, appearing for Defendant Gurjit Singh;
Atty. Michael Choi, Esq. of Wood Smith Henning & Berman LLP, appearing (to observe only) for Defendants Raiser and Uber.

DEFENDANT GURJIT SINGH'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS.

Matter called, NO ARGUMENT is held.

The Court informs Mr. Maxwell that he did not timely oppose the tentative ruling and that opposing counsel is not present. The Court then adopted the tentative ruling as follows:

Specially appearing defendant Gurjit Singh's motion to quash service of summons and to set aside the default entered against him on December 16, 2020 is denied. (Code Civ. Proc., sec. 473(d); see Ellard v. Conway (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 540, 544 [default judgment entered against a defendant who was not served with a summons in the matter prescribed by statute is void].) Pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 415.20(b), plaintiff's process server attempted service on defendant at the Fremont apartment, the address defendant gave as his home address at the scene of the accident, and that still appears on his California driver's license. (See Pls. Opp., Ex. 2; Singh Decl., Ex. A.) There is a presumption of proper service from the declaration of the process server, including the affidavit of reasonable diligence, which reflects that on three occasions, persons in the apartment at that address told the process server that Mr. Singh was "not in at this time" and/or was "at work." (Evid.Code Sec. 647 ["The return of a process server . . . establishes a presumption, affecting the burden of producing evidence, of the facts stated in the return"].) Plaintiff thus establishes that it properly effected substituted service of process at Defendant's "dwelling house or usual place of abode." Defendant's declaration, which reflects on its face that it was executed in India, does not state that it was executed "under penalty of perjury under the laws of the State of California," and is therefore inadmissible. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 2015.5; Bombardier Recreational Products, Inc. v. Dow Chemical Canada ULC (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 591,604 ["Out-of-state declarations that do not state they were made 'under penalty of perjury under the laws of the State of California' (Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 2015.5) are not deemed sufficiently reliable to be admitted into evidence."].) It therefore cannot overcome the presumption of proper service. (See Rodriguez v. Cho (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 742, 750 [defendant required to rebut the presumption of proper service with evidence that he was not so served].)

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and that is in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com. (302/EPS)
